summary: Professional commercial rhythm alternates pressure and release so the viewer feels progression instead of a flat list of features.
principles: Map each beat to a distinct viewer feeling: curiosity, recognition, relief, proof, then action — no two beats should carry the same emotional register.
Use shorter cuts and tighter narration at pressure peaks, then leave breath around product proof — density creates urgency, space creates trust.
Let music intensity and edit density support the emotional curve rather than running at one speed throughout.
The emotional wave should have 2-3 pressure-release cycles in a 30-second ad; a single flat escalation loses viewer engagement by the 20-second mark.
The peak emotional moment should coincide with the product reveal or proof — never waste peak intensity on a transition or branding frame.
Recovery after the peak should be partial, not complete — maintaining some tension into the CTA gives the viewer a reason to act.
Silence and visual stillness are valid emotional states — a beat that drops to quiet calm after high intensity creates a powerful contrast.
Every emotional transition should be motivated by the narrative, not arbitrary — the viewer should feel the shift is earned.
apply_when: The brief asks for emotion, aspiration, premium energy, or a cinematic feeling.
The ad has a clear problem-solution, contrast, or desire-fulfillment arc.
The production includes narration, music, and multiple visual beats.
avoid_when: The ad is a purely informational product listing.
Legal or medical claims require neutral delivery throughout.
The brand tone forbids emotional escalation.
failure_patterns: Flat emotional delivery where every beat has the same intensity — the ad feels like a list of features read aloud.
All pressure, no release: constant high intensity exhausts the viewer and the peak moment has no contrast to stand out against.
Emotional peak in the wrong place: maximum intensity at the branding frame instead of the product reveal.
Emotional curve that drops to zero before the CTA — the viewer has no motivational energy left to act.

cross_domain_notes.note: Music dynamics and tempo are the audible expression of the emotional wave — they must be derived from the same beat sequence and intensity values.
Cut density tracks intensity: held shots at calm, rapid cuts at peak. They are the visual manifestation of the same emotional curve.
The hook's emotional register sets the starting point for the wave — a high-energy hook demands a different escalation path than a quiet one.

execution_techniques: Design the beat sequence with explicit intensity values (0.0-1.0) per beat before writing the script, not after.
Place the highest-intensity beat at the product reveal and ensure the intensity curve peaks there, not earlier.
Build a brief calm beat (intensity 0.2-0.3) before the CTA to let the viewer process, then a slight rise at the CTA itself for action energy.
Use the intensity curve to drive music tempo, editing rhythm, and TTS speed simultaneously for coherent emotional expression.
